coredata.dc:title: Red emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots for fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Cu2+ and biological sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Hongwei
Li, Rui
Wang, Yinxiu
Zhao, Zhonghua
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, red-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared using p-phenylenediamine and polyethylene glycol 20,000 as precursors. The N-CDs are spherical and uniformly distributed with average particle sizes of 2.36 nm. The N-CDs display excitation-independent fluorescence behavior with the optimal excitation and the maximum emission wavelengths centered at 487 and 628 nm, respectively. Results indicated that as prepared CDs can provide a rapid and highly selective recognition of Cu2+ with the range of 45–70 µM and a detection (LOD) limit of 45.87 µM. A clear color change of the N-CDs solution (orange-red to black) can also be visualized by adding Cu2+. Moreover, a smartphone-based platform for the detection of Cu2+ was developed to detect Cu2+ in real time by analyzing RGB values, giving a linear range of 20–140 µM. and a LOD limit of 35.83 µM. In addition, it was applied for the detection of Cu2+ in tap water, the recoveries were 86.18 %-104.95 %, and the relative standard deviations were 1.64 %-2.43 %. The N-CDs can be used to detect Cu2+ in vitro and in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red fluorescence
Carbon dots
Cu2+
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-CDs for fluorescence detection of Cu2+
Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
Cu2+ fluorescence sensing platform based on N-CDs
Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image, inset: HR-TEM image (a) and particle size distribution statistics (b) of the synthesized N-CDs.
XPS spectrum (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) of the prepared N-CDs.

a. UV–vis, optimal excitation, emission spectra (Inset in (a) shows the color of the N-CDs solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)); b. Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of N-CDs. c. UV–vis spectra of relatively high concentrations CDs (5 mg/mL).
Effects of concentration of NaCl (a), continuous xenon lamp irradiation (b) and pH (c) on N-CDs.

a. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs after addition of various concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 140 μM; b. Relationship between F/F0 and Cu2+ concentration, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 628 nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively (Excitation wavelength: 487 nm); c. Photographs of N-CDs added with different concentrations of Cu2+ (from left to right: 0, 20, 43, 60, 70, 90 and 140 μM) under 365 nm UV light; d. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 628 nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+ and other metal ions ((Excitation wavelength: 487 nm. Black bars indicate the addition of ions to the N-CDs solution. The red bar indicates that Cu2+ is continuously added to the system to be tested).

Photographs of N-CDs solution under sunlight after successive addition of Cu2+ (from left to right: 0, 20, 43, 60, 70, 90 and 140 μM), taken directly by smartphone camera (a). Linear regression graph of R/B values against Cu2+ concentration in the ranges of 20–140 μM (b).

Fluorescence decay traces of N-CDs in the absence (red curve) and presence (black curve) of Cu2+ (a). UV absorption spectra of N-CDs upon adding different concentration of Cu2+. The inset shows the change of UV absorption spectra of N-CDs before and after adding the highest concentration of Cu2+ (b).
Zeta potential values of N-CDs and N-CDs/ Cu2+.
Toxicity of N-CDs to HeLa cells.
HeLa cell images of N-CDs responds to Cu2+ (0–180 s).
Toxicity of N-CDs to zebrafish larvae.
Images of N-CDs responds to Cu2+ in zebrafish larvae.
Synthesis of N-CDs and its sensing performance for the detection of Cu2+ in colorimetric and FLUORESCENCE manner.
Comparison of different CDs for the sensing of Cu2+.
Detection of Cu2+ in tap water.

head.ce:title: Red emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots for fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Cu2+ and biological sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, red-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared using p-phenylenediamine and polyethylene glycol 20,000 as precursors. The N-CDs are spherical and uniformly distributed with average particle sizes of 2.36 nm. The N-CDs display excitation-independent fluorescence behavior with the optimal excitation and the maximum emission wavelengths centered at 487 and 628 nm, respectively. Results indicated that as prepared CDs can provide a rapid and highly selective recognition of Cu2+ with the range of 45–70 µM and a detection (LOD) limit of 45.87 µM. A clear color change of the N-CDs solution (orange-red to black) can also be visualized by adding Cu2+. Moreover, a smartphone-based platform for the detection of Cu2+ was developed to detect Cu2+ in real time by analyzing RGB values, giving a linear range of 20–140 µM. and a LOD limit of 35.83 µM. In addition, it was applied for the detection of Cu2+ in tap water, the recoveries were 86.18 %-104.95 %, and the relative standard deviations were 1.64 %-2.43 %. The N-CDs can be used to detect Cu2+ in vitro and in vivo.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ can act as catalytic cofactors for various metalloenzymes, such as superoxide dismutase [1,2], cytochrome c oxidase [3], tyrosinase [4,5] and nucleases [6,7], thereby making itself an essential trace element for organisms [8]. Cu2+ has been widely used in various industrial fields such as chemistry and material science, thus making our life convenient. Appropriate application of Cu2+ can effectively exert antibacterial activity [9]. However, excess Cu2+ in the body may damage DNA [10] and even lead to various serious diseases, such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [11], Alzheimer's disease [12,13], Parkinson's disease [14,15] and Wilson's disease [13,16]. The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the permissible limit for Cu2+ in drinking water at 1.3 ppm (20 μM). Therefore, it is important to monitor Cu2+ levels in an efficient and easy way to ensure our health.

To date, many methods have been proposed to detect Cu2+, including atomic absorption spectroscopy [17], electrochemical assays [18], organic dye-based fluorescence probes [19], and fluorescence or colorimetric-based nanomaterials, etc. methods [20–22]. Among all the above methods, fluorescence sensors based on fluorescence nanomaterials can realize real-time, sensitive, and rapid determination of copper ions, attracting increasing research interest.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the carbon family, are recently becoming as potential replacements for traditional fluorescence materials. Due to its simple preparation process, good chemical stability and excellent biocompatibility, it has outstanding applications in bioimaging and fluorescence sensors. Among the various applications of CDs, water treatment and metal ion sensing in water environment are important applications. Cu2+ can easily enter drinking water if the water passes through a corrosive piping system. Dissolved Cu2+ can make drinking water taste bad. If the contents of Cu2+ in drinking water exceeds the standard, it will cause some health problems for people, such as vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and even liver damage. Therefore, CDs are widely used as probes for the fluorescence detection of Cu2+ [23,24].

However, most of the employed CDs emit in the blue region under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light, which is not suitable for visual detection of Cu2+. Therefore, the preparation of red-emitting CDs for visual detection of Cu2+ is highly desirable because the intensity variation of red is most easily seen by the human eye [25–27]. The reported preparation of excitation-independent red-emitting CDs still suffers from some key issues, such as complex synthesis/isolation procedures [28–31]. Therefore, it is important to develop a simple method to synthesize excitation-independent red-emitting CDs.

In recent years, smartphone-based detection systems have received a lot of attention because they are portable, ubiquitous, and can also produce fast and simple quantitative results. Colorimetric image analysis methods have the advantages of high efficiency and low cost [32]. In this method, images are divided into their main red, green and blue components (RGB). Compared with traditional qualitative analysis methods, digital image colorimetry based on portable devices can facilitate real-time on-site quantitative analysis [33].

In this work, a kind of N-CDs with excitation-independent properties (emission wavelength 628 nm) were rapidly prepared by a hydrothermal method using p-phenylenediamine and polyethylene glycol 20,000 as precursors. N-CDs were used for FLUORESCENCE and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Cu2+ (Scheme 1 ). The linear range is 45–70 Μm and the limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ fluorescence sensor is 45.87 μM, which can be used to confirm the contents of Cu2+ in tap water. The recovery rate in the standard addition test is 86.18 %-104.95 %, relative standard deviation is 1.64 %-2.43 %. In addition, a smartphone-based platform for the detection of Cu2+ was developed to detect Cu2+ in real time by analyzing RGB values, with an LOD of 35.83 µM. It is worth mentioning that the N-CDs can be used to detect Cu2+ in vitro and in vivo.

697.61 mg of polyethylene glycol 20,000 and 108.14 mg of p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, placed the solution in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor liner, put it into the corresponding reactor, and put it in the heated in an oven at 200 °C for 10 h. Then, the reaction kettle was cooled to room temperature, and the solution was poured into a centrifuge tube. After centrifugation for 33 min at 3000 rpm, the supernatant was taken and filtered with a 0.45 μm water-based filter. It was then lyophilized to obtain a solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs for fluorescence detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of Cu2+ (0 to 140 μM) were added to a mixture of 50 μL N-CDs solution (23.4 mg/mL) and 1000 μL PBS buffer (pH 7.0), respectively. The selectivity of N-CDs to Cu2+ was evaluated by adding 1 μL of other metal ion solutions (0.1 mol/L) in place of Cu2+ in a similar manner. The anti-interference ability was evaluated by simultaneously adding Cu2+ to one of the other metal ion solutions in a similar manner.

Based on the colorimetric quantitative analysis of Cu2+ by smartphone, the N-CDs working solution was added to a glass bottle, and 0, 20, 43, 60, 70, 90, and 140 μM of Cu2+ solution were added, respectively, and stirred evenly. Fix the iphone7 to the phone holder, take a photo under FLUORESCENCE light, directly analyze the displayed photo images using the ColorPicker app installed on the smartphone, record the RGB values, repeat six times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then, 1000 μL of tap water and 50 μL of 23.4 mg/mL N-CDs solution were mixed, and the fluorescence was measured to record the F0 value. Then 48, 52 and 56 µM Cu2+ solutions were added, and the fluorescence intensity was measured to record the F value. The above operation was repeated six times in order to calculate the relative standard deviation. Calculate the content of Cu2+ according to the working curve, and calculate the recovery rate of standard addition.

The morphology and size distribution of N-CDs were known by TEM. The synthesized N-CDs were monodispersed and uniform in size (Fig. 1 a). The high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 1a) demonstrates the lattice fringe of N-CDs is approximately 0.25 nm, which is close to the diffraction plane (100) of graphitic carbon. Their size distribution closely matched the Gaussian distribution (120 N-CDs were measured), with a statistical mean diameter of 2.36 nm and a particle size distribution range of 1.33–3.53 nm (Fig. 1b).

XPS spectroscopy provides more convincing evidence for the elemental composition and surface functional groups of as-prepared N-CDs. The full-range XPS (Fig. 3a) spectrum clearly shows three peaks at 285.56, 399.67, and 530.16 eV, ascribed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. This further confirms that prepared N-CDs mainly contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) is decomposed into four characteristic peaks at 284.12 eV (CC), 285.37 eV (C-OH/COC), 283.48 eV (CC) and 285.05 eV (CN/CO). The 398.03 eV of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) corresponds to the amino N. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) contains two peaks at 531.48 eV and 531.28 eV, indicating the possible presence of C-OH and COC. The surface composition of N-CDs determined by XPS was consistent with the results of FT-IR. FT-IR and XPS results indicated that nitrogen was doped in N-CDs, and related functional groups such as hydroxyl and amino groups existed on the surface.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to study the optical properties of N-CDs. The UV–vis spectrum has two obvious absorption peaks at 236 nm and 298 nm, which may be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain (blue line in Fig. 4 a). In addition, the spectrum has a broad tail band in the range from 400 nm to 600 nm, corresponding to n-π* edge transition. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 4c. The red and black lines in Fig. 4a represent the optimal excitation and maximum emission wavelengths of N-CDs at 487 nm and 628 nm, respectively. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 460 nm to 540 nm, their emission maxima are all located at 628 nm, indicating that the emission wavelength is excitation-independent, which may be attributed to the relatively uniform surface state and size distribution. The strongest emission intensity (QY = 2.16 %) was obtained when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 485 nm, which was almost identical to the Ex scan (487 nm).

The stability of the prepared N-CDs under various conditions was investigated. Under the high ionic strength of 3 M, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs did not change significantly (Fig. 5 a). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs hardly changed after 600 s of continuous irradiation (Fig. 5b). Subsequently, the fluorescence signals of N-CDs at different pH values were recorded (Fig. 5c), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under alkaline conditions was higher than that under acidic conditions, but the fluorescence intensity decreased not significantly at pH 2–6, showing that N-CDs have good acid and alkali resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ fluorescence sensing platform based on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the sensing properties of N-CDs for Cu2+ were investigated. The fluorescence intensity at 628 nm gradually decreases with increasing Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 6 a). Fig. 6b shows the relationship between the quenching efficiency (F/F0) and the Cu2+ concentration, and the curve is fitted with the Boltzmann function (R2 = 0.99007). When the concentration of Cu2+ is in the range of 45–70 μM, there is a linear relationship between Cu2+ concentration and F/F0 (R2 = 0.99204). The detection limit is 45.87 μM (S/N = 3). Fig. 6c shows the photographs of N-CDs solutions after adding different concentrations of Cu2+ under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm). With the increase of Cu2+ concentration (0–140 μM), the red fluorescence of N-CDs was gradually quenched.

In addition, the selectivity and anti-interference experiments for Cu2+ sensing were carried out. The effects of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity response (F/F0) of N-CDs were analyzed respectively (Fig. 6d), and it is found that only after adding Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity response (F/F0) of N-CDs decreases significantly, indicating that the N-CDs have good selectivity to Cu2+. Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 6d that in the presence of other metal ions, N-CDs still have good selectivity to Cu2+, that is, strong anti-interference.

Interestingly, after adding different concentrations of Cu2+, the color of the N-CDs solution gradually changed from red to black, which can be observed by naked eye under natural light (Fig. 8 a). The colorimetric signal of the N-CDs/Cu2+ system is captured by the smartphone iphone7 camera, and the images are processed by the internal Color Picker application (app). Through this app, the RGB values of the images can be obtained. The color change of N-CDs induced by Cu2+ in the range of 20–140 µM was quantified by RGB analysis. A good linear regression equation between the R/B value and Cu2+ concentration is obtained (Fig. 8b): R/B = -0.0142 CCu 2++4.3430 (R2 = 0.96213), the detection limit is 35.83 µM (S/N = 3). It provides a strategy for portable on-site quantitative monitoring of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the prepared N-CDs for the determination of Cu2+ in tap water was further investigated by standard addition recovery experiments. As shown in Table 2 , the measured Cu2+ concentration in tap water is basically consistent with the added Cu2+ concentration, the recovery rate is between 86.18 % and 104.95 %, and the relative standard deviation is between 1.64 % and 2.43 %. Therefore, the N-CDs can be used to detect Cu2+ in tap water.

In this study, red-emitting N-CDs were prepared from p-phenylenediamine and polyethylene glycol 20000. The N-CDs have good chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Based on the static quenching mechanism, N-CDs can detect Cu2+ by fluorescence with an LOD of 45.87 µM (S/N = 3) and a detection range of 45–70 µM. More importantly, N-CDs have obvious color changes under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm), so we captured RGB values with a smartphone app with a linear range of 20–140 µM and an LOD of 35.83 µM. Surprisingly, the N-CDs can be applied not only to the detection of Cu2+ in tap water, but also to detect Cu2+ in vitro and in vivo.

Hongwei Zhao: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Rui Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Yinxiu Wang: Writing, Data curation. Zhonghua Zhao: Software, Conceptualization. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114575.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
